segments.caption: The video opens with a vintage filmstrip effect, displaying the date 'May 20, 1998' over a grainy football match, setting a nostalgic tone. The scene transitions to a celebratory moment where a Real Madrid player in a white jersey lifts the Champions League trophy, surrounded by teammates in jubilation, with the year '1998' prominently displayed. The celebration continues with a close-up of a man in formal attire, later identified as a club official, holding the trophy alongside a player, both smiling. The focus then shifts to a close-up of the same man, now smoking a cigar, with the Real Madrid crest visible in the background, suggesting a moment of triumph or reflection. On-screen text reveals the club's 'Total Debt €278M', sourced from UEFA, juxtaposing success with financial strain. The scene changes to a wide shot of the empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium, with 'REAL MADRID C.F.' spelled out in the stands, emphasizing the club's grandeur. The final frame displays 'Average Attendance 99/00: 34241 Fans per match', sourced from Transfermarkt.com, highlighting a decline in fan engagement despite the trophy win, all framed within the consistent filmstrip animation.

segments.caption: The video opens with a black-and-white archival photograph of a Real Madrid team group, including players and officials, posing with a trophy, evoking a sense of historical legacy. The scene transitions to a close-up of Santiago Bernabéu, identified by on-screen text, smiling beside another man in formal attire, suggesting a moment of leadership and camaraderie. The Real Madrid crest appears over a vintage photo of players crouching with a ball, reinforcing the club’s identity. The narrative then shifts to modern times, showing a portrait of Di Stefano labeled as 'Honorary President,' followed by a younger Luis Figo holding up a Real Madrid jersey with his name, symbolizing a pivotal transfer. The video highlights Figo’s image rights with a graphic showing '50%' split, indicating a financial agreement. The focus then turns to Florentino Pérez, the man in formal attire with glasses and a patterned tie, seen on the phone beside a large 'GANA en MADRID' sign, suggesting his campaign for presidency. A newspaper clipping reveals '46.200 millones de deuda' (46.2 million debt), while another headline reads 'Guerra en el Madrid' (War in Madrid), illustrating internal conflict. The narrative continues with a close-up of Fernando Redondo, followed by a black-and-white image of fans holding protest signs against his sale, with text like 'REDONDO NO SE VENDE' (Redondo is not for sale). The final frame shows a quote from a book, 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid,' explaining how Redondo’s public support for Sanz created enemies with the new president, Pérez, underscoring the political and emotional tensions within the club during this period.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of Real Madrid’s 2000-01 season, beginning with a match highlight from January 2001 where Madrid defeats Valencia 1-0, followed by a graphic displaying the 2000 Ballon d’Or results with Luis Figo winning, Zidane second, and others trailing. The scene transitions to a newspaper-style graphic announcing 'El Madrid se va' (Madrid is leaving) alongside a 2-2 draw against Barcelona in March 2001, with a league table showing Real Madrid leading. A tactical diagram appears, illustrating the attacking roles of Raúl and Guti, with a close-up of Vicente del Bosque, the manager, observing intently. The diagram then shifts to show Makélélé and Helguera in midfield, followed by a moment of camaraderie between Helguera and a player in a red and yellow jersey. The narrative continues with a tense moment on the pitch, where Real Madrid players, including Figo, appear dejected after a match, possibly following a loss. A quote from Steve McManaman overlays the scene, reflecting on the team’s overconfidence and lack of detailed video analysis, suggesting a shift in mindset or strategy. The atmosphere is one of transition, introspection, and the weight of expectations during a pivotal season for Real Madrid.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of Steve McManaman in profile, wearing a white Real Madrid jersey with dark blue trim, as a quote overlay discusses the team’s minimal video analysis, setting a tone of overconfidence. The scene transitions to archival footage of a 2001 Champions League semifinal, showing a goal scored by Bayern Munich against Real Madrid, with on-screen text identifying the match as '00/01 CL Semifinal – 1st Leg MADRID 0-1 BAYERN MAY – 2001'. The footage continues with a second goal, now labeled '00/01 CL Semifinal – 2nd Leg BAYERN 2-1 MADRID MAY – 2001', followed by jubilant Bayern fans celebrating. The narrative shifts to a newspaper clipping with the headline 'FLORENTINO HOPES TO CLEAR DEBT' dated '8 MAY, 2001', featuring Florentino Pérez in a suit, symbolizing financial struggles. This is followed by a graphic of the 'CIUDAD DEPORTIVA, MADRID', showing a transformation from 'GREEN LAND' to 'CONSTRUCTABLE AREA' via an arrow, indicating urban development. The video then cuts to a 5-0 La Liga victory over Alavés, with a Real Madrid player in a white jersey celebrating a goal, followed by a scene of players lifting the La Liga trophy amidst falling confetti. A newspaper clipping titled 'Carta blanca' appears, with Florentino Pérez’s face superimposed, suggesting his growing influence. The segment concludes with a Google Earth satellite view of the 'OLD TRAINING GROUND' in Madrid, overlaid with the cost '€446.7 MILLION', emphasizing the financial scale of the club’s redevelopment plans.

segments.caption: The video opens with a formal event where Florentino Pérez, in a suit and tie, stands beside Zinedine Zidane, who is holding up a white Real Madrid jersey with 'ZIDANE 5' printed on it, symbolizing his official unveiling. The scene transitions to a young boy in a Real Madrid jersey, excitedly holding a similar jersey, followed by a graphic overlay showing Zidane giving a thumbs-up with text indicating 'Image Rights 20%' and 'Salary €5M/YR After Tax'. The narrative shifts to Pérez signing autographs for fans, including a young boy, in a lively, crowded setting. This is followed by a sequence of animated graphics: the Real Madrid crest evolving from 1902 to 2002, accompanied by a quote from Carlos Pinedo stating, 'Winning the club's first treble in our hundred-year history is our grand objective.' The video then cuts to grainy black-and-white archival footage of a 1960 match at Hampden Park, followed by a quote from Luis Figo: 'We believe winning in Glasgow is sort of our destiny.' The segment concludes with a close-up of a man with a mustache, likely Vicente del Bosque, looking thoughtful, with a black bar obscuring the lower part of the frame.

segments.caption: The video opens with a black-and-white montage of a man with a mustache, appearing distressed and holding his head, symbolizing a crisis, followed by the bold text 'Problema Zidane'—indicating Zidane as the central issue. The scene transitions to a split-screen showing Zidane in a French national team jersey on the left and in a Juventus jersey on the right, with tactical diagrams behind him, suggesting his dual identity and influence. A league table is then shown, highlighting Real Madrid’s poor form with only 5 points from 15 games, emphasized by the text 'Madrid se tambalea' (Madrid is wobbling). A graphic of Zidane holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'ZIDANE 5' appears, reinforcing the transfer narrative. The video then shifts to a Champions League form graphic showing Salgado with four consecutive wins against Roma, Loko Moscow, and Anderlecht, contrasting Real Madrid’s domestic struggles with their European success. The scene cuts to live-action footage of Zidane in a Juventus jersey, dribbling and scoring, showcasing his skill. This is followed by a Real Madrid player in a white jersey scoring a goal, with the text 'Cambio de líder' (Change of leader) and a league table showing Real Madrid at the top with 35 points, indicating a turnaround. The final shot is a goal by Figo in a 2-0 win over Barcelona, with on-screen text '01/02 La Liga – GW11 MADRID 2-0 BARCELONA FIGO – 90+2', marking a dramatic late victory.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dramatic goal by Figo in a La Liga match, scoring in the 90+2 minute to secure a 2-0 victory for Real Madrid over Barcelona, highlighted by on-screen text. This is followed by a black-and-white newspaper clipping with the headline 'Otro batacazo blanco' (Another white blow), emphasizing the significance of the win, and a league table showing Real Madrid in third place with 40 points, just behind Valencia and Celta. The scene transitions to the RFEF logo and a title card for the 2002 Copa del Rey Final on March 6, 2002, with a stylized Real Madrid centenary logo. The Santiago Bernabéu stadium is shown at night, illuminated, setting the stage for the final. A filmstrip montage follows, showcasing past Copa del Rey winners: Valencia (1999), Espanyol (2000), and Zaragoza (2001), each with players lifting the trophy. The 2002 final between Real Madrid and Deportivo is then shown, with Deportivo winning 2-1, as players in blue and white celebrate with the trophy. The video then shifts to a newspaper headline 'Igualdad total' (Total equality), followed by a league table showing Real Madrid tied with Barcelona at 39 points. The final scene shows Real Madrid players lining up for a Champions League quarter-final match against Bayern Munich in April 2002, with Zidane in focus, wearing his white jersey with 'ZIDANE 5' on the back, as the camera captures his intense expression before the match.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dramatic sequence from the 2001/02 Champions League quarter-finals, showing Bayern Munich defeating Real Madrid 2-1 in the first leg, with a Bayern player celebrating a goal and the scoreboard confirming the result. The scene shifts to Real Madrid’s comeback in the second leg, where Guti scores in the 85th minute to equalize 2-2 on aggregate, with the on-screen text indicating a 3-2 aggregate win for Madrid. The narrative then transitions to the semi-finals, where Real Madrid defeats Barcelona 2-0 in April 2002, with a player in a white jersey scoring and celebrating. The mood turns somber as a graphic titled 'Caída estrepitosa' (Catastrophic fall) appears, listing Osasuna, Sociedad, and Deportivo, hinting at Real Madrid’s subsequent struggles. The video then cuts to a celebratory moment with Real Madrid players lifting the Champions League trophy amidst confetti, followed by training footage of players including Zidane and a coach in a red jacket. The final frames show a filmstrip-style montage of a player in a red jersey with 'Bundesliga Runners-up' and 'DFB Pokal Runners-up' text, suggesting a period of near-misses and disappointment for Bayern Munich, contrasting with Real Madrid’s earlier triumphs.

segments.caption: The video opens with a filmstrip-style montage of a player in a red and black Bayer Leverkusen jersey, labeled 'DFB Pokal Runners-up,' setting a tone of near-miss achievement. The scene transitions to the 2002 Champions League Final between Leverkusen and Real Madrid, with on-screen text marking the date: May 15, 2002. The match unfolds with Real Madrid taking an early lead as Raúl scores in the 9th minute, followed by a Leverkusen equalizer from Lúcio in the 14th minute. The momentum shifts again as Zidane scores for Real Madrid in the 45th minute, putting them ahead 2-1. The footage captures the intensity of the match, including close-ups of players, goal celebrations, and a goalkeeper’s reaction. The sequence culminates in Real Madrid lifting the Champions League trophy in triumph, with players in white jerseys celebrating on the podium. The video then cuts to a black screen with the text '02-03 Season,' followed by a split-screen interview graphic featuring two men in suits, one of whom is identified as Carlos Pinedo, with a quote in Spanish: 'No veo fichajes, no hay stars' (I don’t see signings, there are no stars), referencing Real Madrid’s transfer strategy post-victory. The atmosphere shifts from the high-energy match to a more analytical, reflective tone as the narrative moves into the next season.

segments.caption: The video opens with a black-and-white grid background, displaying a quote in Spanish: 'No veo fichajes, no hay stars' (I don’t see signings, there are no stars), attributed to Carlos Pinedo, who appears in a suit and tie with a serious expression. The scene transitions to a composite image of Pinedo flanked by two Real Madrid players, Campo and Karanka, labeled with their names. Next, the focus shifts to Makélélé, shown in a white Real Madrid jersey, with a text overlay calling him the 'MOST IMPORTANT PLAYER ON THE FIELD.' This is followed by a side-by-side comparison of Makélélé and Zidane, with their respective salaries displayed: €1.2M/year for Makélélé and €5M/year for Zidane. The narrative then introduces Florentino Pérez, a man in a suit and tie with glasses, who appears against a grid background. Above him, the phrase 'Zidanes Y Pavones' (Zidanes and Peacocks) appears in red and black text, symbolizing a dual strategy of signing superstars and academy players. A series of images then appear in stacked rows: the top row labeled 'CRACS' (stars) features Zidane, Figo, Ronaldo, and others; the middle row, labeled '...', shows additional players; and the bottom row, labeled 'CANTERA' (academy), displays younger players. The final frame features a quote from EL PAÍS: 'La política de 'zidanes y pavones' elimina a los de clase media' (The 'Zidanes and peacocks' policy eliminates the middle class), overlaid on an image of a Real Madrid player in a white jersey, with his eyes obscured by a black bar, emphasizing the critique of the club’s transfer policy.

The scene transitions to a close-up of Florentino Pérez, smiling as he signs an autograph at a G-14 event, symbolizing his influence in European football. The video then presents a split-screen with Pérez and Moratti, connected by a red 'X' over an arrow, symbolizing a failed deal, while a third panel shows a man in a suit (likely a commentator or club official) gesturing seriously. The final frames introduce 'Opción Morientes' with a black-and-white image of Morientes in a Real Madrid training kit, juxtaposed with a newspaper article, suggesting him as an alternative signing. The sequence ends with a return to the split-screen, now with Morientes’s image replacing the financial figure, reinforcing the narrative of transfer strategy and missed opportunities.

segments.caption: The video opens with a black-and-white newspaper graphic titled 'Ronaldo, negado como titular,' displaying a table of Ronaldo’s recent match statistics, showing 0 goals in 435 minutes across five games, with 10 shots on goal, highlighting his struggles as a starter. The scene transitions to a split-screen: on the left, a newspaper headline in Spanish reads 'Pitos en el Bernabéu al equipo blanco y a Ronaldo,' with an English translation overlay 'Whistles in the Bernabéu aimed at the blancos & Ronaldo,' while the right side shows Ronaldo in a white Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11' on the back, walking dejectedly. The footage then cuts to a match between Barcelona and Madrid in November 2002, with Ronaldo in a white jersey with 'SIEMENS mobile' sponsorship, visibly frustrated as he walks off the field after a 0-0 draw, with fans in the background holding a banner reading 'CATALUNYA NO OBLIDA JUDE$!!'—a reference to Luis Figo’s controversial move to Real Madrid. The scene shifts to a 2002 Intercontinental Cup match, where Ronaldo scores a goal against Olimpia, celebrating with teammates, including Figo, who is seen in a white jersey with 'FIGO 10' on the back. The video then shows Ronaldo holding the 2002 Ballon d'Or trophy, with a graphic listing his 161 votes, ahead of R. Carlos, Kahn, Zidane, and Ballack. The final frames depict Ronaldo in training, wearing a black jacket over a white jersey, and then in action during a match, scoring a goal against a team in blue and white, followed by a close-up of his jersey with 'RONALDO 11' as he celebrates. The segment ends with a graphic of the Champions League quarterfinals, showing Manchester United vs. Real Madrid, with images of Sir Alex Ferguson and Vicente del Bosque on either side, setting up the next phase of the narrative.

The footage shifts to players entering the pitch, including a close-up of Ronaldo in a black Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11' on the back, as he scores three goals in quick succession, each marked with on-screen text showing the score progression: 0-1, 1-2, and 2-3. segments.caption: The video opens with a filmstrip-style montage of Real Madrid players in white jerseys with 'SIEMENS' sponsorship, visibly dejected in the rain after a 2-1 loss to Mallorca on February 26, 2000, setting a tone of disappointment. This transitions to a close-up of the La Liga table, highlighting Real Madrid’s 48 points, third place, and the growing gap to Barcelona’s 58 points, emphasizing the team’s struggles. The scene shifts to a black-and-white newspaper graphic with the headline 'EL FINAL DE LA ERA GALÁCTICA' and a large 'DIMISIÓN' (Resignation) over a man in a suit and tie, identified as Florentino Pérez, who is quoted as saying 'He spoiled a los jugadores' (He spoiled the players). The narrative continues with a close-up of a man in a dark suit, later identified as José Mourinho, speaking into a microphone with the quote 'From now on, I will play with players not names,' suggesting a shift in philosophy. The video then cuts to a graphic of Mourinho shouting at a grid, listing teams like Valencia, Betis, Zaragoza, Barca, Sociedad, and Getafe, indicating a series of draws. This is followed by archival footage of a Real Madrid player in a white jersey scoring against Barcelona, then a dejected Real Madrid player after a 1-1 draw with Sociedad in April 2006. The final scene shows a bald man in a white Real Madrid jersey, identified as Zinedine Zidane, at a press conference with the quote 'Au revoir' and the date April 25, 2006, marking his departure. The overall atmosphere is one of decline, resignation, and transition, underscored by stark visuals and critical quotes.
